description: NuScale FSAR Ch.01/Ch.05 multi-evidence QA benchmark for RAG system evaluation

source_documents.name: NuScale FSAR Ch.01 - Introduction and General Description
NuScale FSAR Ch.05 - Reactor Coolant System

reasoning_types.comparative: Compare/contrast values or features (How does X relate to Y?)
reasoning_types.judgment: Regulatory judgment or synthesis (Does X satisfy requirement Y? What is the impact of X?)

complexity_levels.multi_evidence: Requires 2-3 evidence pieces from same chapter
complexity_levels.cross_document: Requires evidence from both Ch.01 and Ch.05

questions.question: What is the total net electrical output of a full 12-module NuScale plant?
questions.expected_answer: The total net electrical output of a full NuScale plant with 12 modules is approximately 570 MWe.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: The NuScale plant design consists of up to 12 NuScale Power Modules with a total capacity of 570 MWe.

questions.question: What is the operating pressure of the NuScale Reactor Coolant System?
questions.expected_answer: The NuScale RCS operates at 1850 psia.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: RCS operating pressure is 1850 psia.

questions.question: What type of steam generator does NuScale use?
questions.expected_answer: NuScale uses helical coil steam generators integrated within the reactor pressure vessel.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: The NPM uses helical coil steam generators.

questions.question: What is the fuel type used in the NuScale reactor?
questions.expected_answer: NuScale uses UO2 (uranium dioxide) fuel in a standard 17x17 lattice configuration.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: The fuel is standard UO2 in a 17x17 fuel assembly lattice.

questions.question: What is the design life of a NuScale Power Module?
questions.expected_answer: The NuScale Power Module has a 60-year design life.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: NuScale design life is 60 years.

questions.question: What is the refueling interval for the NuScale reactor?
questions.expected_answer: The NuScale reactor has a 24-month refueling cycle.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Refueling interval is 24 months.

questions.question: What thermodynamic cycle does the NuScale plant use for power conversion?
questions.expected_answer: NuScale uses a conventional Rankine cycle for power conversion.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: The power conversion cycle is a conventional Rankine cycle.

questions.question: What is the design pressure of the NuScale reactor pressure vessel?
questions.expected_answer: The RPV design pressure is 2100 psia.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: The RPV design pressure is 2100 psia.

questions.question: What is the cladding thickness on the NuScale RPV interior?
questions.expected_answer: The RPV interior cladding thickness is 0.25 inches.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: The RPV clad thickness is 0.25 inches.

questions.question: How many reactor safety valves does the NuScale RCS have?
questions.expected_answer: The NuScale RCS has 2 reactor safety valves (RSVs).

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Two reactor safety valves are provided.

questions.question: What is the reactor pool temperature limit during DHRS operation?
questions.expected_answer: The reactor pool temperature limit is 140 degrees F.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Reactor pool temperature limit: 140 degrees F.

questions.question: What is the containment vessel design for NuScale?
questions.expected_answer: NuScale uses a vacuum containment vessel (CNV) that operates at near-vacuum conditions during normal operation.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: The containment vessel operates under vacuum conditions.

questions.question: According to Table 1.2-1, what is the thermal power rating of each NuScale Power Module?
questions.expected_answer: According to Table 1.2-1, each NuScale Power Module has a thermal power rating of 160 MWt.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Table 1.2-1: Thermal power per module: 160 MWt.

questions.question: According to Table 5.1-2, what is the hot leg temperature at 100% power?
questions.expected_answer: Table 5.1-2 shows the hot leg temperature (THot) at 100% power is 590.1 degrees F.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Table 5.1-2: THot at 100% power = 590.1°F.

questions.question: According to Table 5.1-2, what is the cold leg temperature at 100% power?
questions.expected_answer: Table 5.1-2 shows the cold leg temperature (TCold) at 100% power is 496.6 degrees F.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Table 5.1-2: TCold at 100% power = 496.6°F.

questions.question: What is the RCS primary flow rate at 100% power according to Table 5.1-2?
questions.expected_answer: According to Table 5.1-2, the primary flow rate at 100% power is 587 kg/s.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Table 5.1-2: Primary flow rate at 100% power = 587 kg/s.

questions.question: What is the hot leg volume according to Table 5.1-1?
questions.expected_answer: According to Table 5.1-1, the hot leg volume is 635 ft³.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Table 5.1-1: Hot Leg volume = 635 ft³.

questions.question: According to Table 5.2-2, what is the RSV capacity in lbm/hr?
questions.expected_answer: Table 5.2-2 states the RSV capacity is 63,360 lbm/hr.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Table 5.2-2: RSV capacity = 63360 lbm/hr.

questions.question: What is the pressurizer volume according to Table 5.1-1?
questions.expected_answer: According to Table 5.1-1, the pressurizer volume is 578 ft³.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Table 5.1-1: PZR volume = 578 ft³.

questions.question: What are the RSV setpoints according to Table 5.2-2?
questions.expected_answer: According to Table 5.2-2, the RSV setpoints are 2075 psia and 2100 psia.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Table 5.2-2: RSV setpoints are 2075 psia and 2100 psia.

questions.question: Based on Figure 1.2-4, what are the main external components visible on the NuScale Power Module?
questions.expected_answer: Figure 1.2-4 shows the NuScale Power Module with the containment vessel as the outermost structure, the reactor pressure vessel inside, and connections for steam and feedwater lines at the top.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Figure 1.2-4: NuScale Power Module showing CNV, RPV, and piping connections.

questions.question: What does Figure 5.1-1 show about the major components of the NuScale Power Module?
questions.expected_answer: Figure 5.1-1 illustrates the major components including the RPV, containment vessel, pressurizer region at the top, steam generator section in the middle, and reactor core at the bottom.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Figure 5.1-1: NuScale Power Module Major Components.

questions.question: What flow path is depicted in Figure 5.1-2, the RCS Simplified Diagram?
questions.expected_answer: Figure 5.1-2 shows the simplified RCS flow path: coolant rises through the core (hot leg/riser), passes through the pressurizer region, flows down through the steam generator (cold leg/downcomer), and returns to the core inlet, driven by natural circulation.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Figure 5.1-2: RCS Simplified Diagram showing natural circulation flow path.

questions.question: What does Figure 1.2-1 depict about the NuScale plant site layout?
questions.expected_answer: Figure 1.2-1 shows the conceptual site layout of the NuScale plant, including the reactor building housing the reactor pool with up to 12 modules, the turbine building, cooling towers, and other support structures.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Figure 1.2-1: Conceptual Site Layout.

questions.question: What is the NuScale pressurizer volume, and how does the pressurizer region appear in Figure 5.4-17?
questions.expected_answer: Table 5.1-1 gives the pressurizer volume as 578 ft³. Figure 5.4-17 shows the pressurizer is an integral part of the upper RPV head, with no separate vessel, using heaters and spray for pressure control.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Table 5.1-1: PZR volume = 578 ft³.

questions.question: What is the steam generator configuration shown in Figure 5.4-1, and what are its key design parameters from Table 5.4-1?
questions.expected_answer: Figure 5.4-1 shows the helical coil steam generator assembly located within the RPV annulus between the riser and RPV wall. Table 5.4-1 specifies design pressure of 2100 psia, design temperature of 650 degrees F, steam outlet temperature of approximately 575 degrees F, and feedwater inlet temperature of approximately 300 degrees F.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Figure 5.4-1: Steam Generator Assembly showing helical coil configuration.

questions.question: What material is used for the RPV shell, and what chemical composition limits apply to the beltline region according to Table 5.3-2?
questions.expected_answer: The RPV shell is fabricated from SA-508 Grade 3 Class 1 low-alloy steel (Table 5.2-4). Table 5.3-2 limits beltline chemistry to: S <= 0.010%, P <= 0.010%, Cu <= 0.06%, Ni <= 0.85% to minimize radiation embrittlement.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Table 5.2-4: RPV shell material is SA-508 Grade 3 Class 1.

questions.question: What does the RCS Schematic Flow Diagram (Figure 5.1-3) show, and what are the corresponding temperature values from Table 5.1-2?
questions.expected_answer: Figure 5.1-3 shows the RCS schematic with flow paths through the core, riser, SG, and downcomer with instrument locations. Table 5.1-2 provides THot=590.1 degrees F, TCold=496.6 degrees F, and Tavg=543.3 degrees F at 100% power.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Figure 5.1-3: RCS Schematic Flow Diagram.

questions.question: What is the RPV cross section configuration shown in Figure 5.3-1, and what are the vessel inner diameter dimensions from Table 5.3-1?
questions.expected_answer: Figure 5.3-1 shows the RPV cross section with the core region, downcomer annulus, and vessel wall with cladding. Table 5.3-1 specifies inner diameters of 96.5 inches (lower), 104.5 inches (middle), and 106.5 inches (upper).

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Figure 5.3-1: RPV Cross Section.

questions.question: What does Figure 5.2-1 show about the reactor safety valve, and what are the RSV design parameters from Table 5.2-2?
questions.expected_answer: Figure 5.2-1 shows the physical configuration of the reactor safety valve mounted on the RPV head. Table 5.2-2 specifies 2 RSVs with 2100 psia design pressure, 63,360 lbm/hr capacity each, and setpoints of 2075/2100 psia.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Figure 5.2-1: Reactor Safety Valve configuration.

questions.question: Describe the natural circulation mechanism in the NuScale RCS and the role of the riser in establishing flow.
questions.expected_answer: The NuScale RCS uses natural circulation without reactor coolant pumps. Heated coolant rises through the hot leg riser due to density differences between hot and cold fluid. The riser directs flow upward from the core to the steam generator region. Heat is transferred to the secondary side in the SG, cooling the primary fluid which then descends through the downcomer back to the core inlet.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: The RCS operates by natural circulation without reactor coolant pumps.
The riser directs heated coolant upward from the core to the SG region.

questions.question: Describe the DHRS function and its passive cooling mechanism.
questions.expected_answer: The Decay Heat Removal System provides passive cooling following a reactor trip. Each NPM has two independent DHRS trains connected to the SG secondary side. DHRS condensers are submerged in the reactor pool, transferring decay heat from the RCS through the SG tubes to the pool water by natural circulation on both primary and secondary sides.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: DHRS provides passive decay heat removal via condensers in the reactor pool.
Each NPM has two independent DHRS trains connected to the SG secondary side.

questions.question: Explain how the NuScale containment vessel design differs from conventional containments and its role in safety.
questions.expected_answer: The NuScale CNV operates under vacuum conditions during normal operation, unlike conventional pressurized containments. This vacuum reduces heat transfer from RPV to containment and provides a pressure suppression function. During a LOCA, steam released into the CNV condenses on the cool inner wall, maintaining low containment pressure without active spray systems.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: The containment vessel operates under vacuum conditions.
Steam condensation on the CNV inner wall provides passive pressure suppression.

questions.question: What are the ECCS valve types and their actuation mechanism in the NuScale design?
questions.expected_answer: The ECCS consists of reactor vent valves (RVVs) and reactor recirculation valves (RRVs). RVVs connect the RPV to the CNV above the waterline, and RRVs connect below. Both valve types are actuated by an inadvertent actuation block (IAB) that prevents spurious opening at normal operating pressure, and they fail open on loss of power.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: ECCS consists of reactor vent valves and reactor recirculation valves.
IAB prevents spurious ECCS actuation at normal operating pressure. Valves fail open on loss of power.

questions.question: What is the pressurizer design in NuScale, and how does it control RCS pressure?
questions.expected_answer: The NuScale pressurizer is an integral part of the upper RPV head, not a separate vessel. It uses electric heaters to increase pressure and a spray system to reduce pressure. The pressurizer volume accommodates RCS inventory changes during transients while maintaining pressure within the operating band.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: The pressurizer is integral to the upper RPV head.
Pressure control uses electric heaters and spray.

questions.question: What is the chemical and volume control system (CVCS) role in the NuScale RCS?
questions.expected_answer: The CVCS maintains RCS water chemistry by controlling boron concentration, removing corrosion products and fission products via ion exchangers and filters, and managing RCS inventory. It provides makeup water and letdown flow, and adjusts boron concentration for reactivity control during the fuel cycle.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: CVCS controls boron concentration and maintains water chemistry.
CVCS provides makeup water, letdown flow, and ion exchange purification.

questions.question: How does the NuScale LTOP system protect the RCS during low-temperature conditions?
questions.expected_answer: Low Temperature Overpressure Protection prevents RCS pressure from exceeding limits when the reactor is at low temperatures and vessel ductility is reduced. The LTOP system uses relief valves with temperature-dependent setpoints. Multiple setpoint ranges are defined based on RCS temperature to ensure the pressure-temperature limits are not exceeded during heatup and cooldown.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: LTOP prevents exceeding pressure limits at low RCS temperatures.
LTOP uses temperature-dependent setpoints for relief valves during heatup/cooldown.

questions.question: What corrosion-resistant materials are used for RCS pressure boundary components in contact with primary coolant?
questions.expected_answer: Primary coolant-wetted surfaces use corrosion-resistant materials. The RPV interior is clad with stainless steel or nickel-based alloy. Steam generator tubes are Alloy 690, chosen for stress corrosion cracking resistance. Bolting uses Alloy 718 for high-temperature strength and corrosion resistance.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: SG tubes are Alloy 690 thermally treated for SCC resistance.
Bolting material is Alloy 718. RPV interior surfaces are clad with corrosion-resistant material.

questions.question: What inservice inspection capabilities does the NuScale RPV design provide?
questions.expected_answer: The NuScale RPV design provides access for volumetric and surface examination of the vessel shell, welds, and nozzles per ASME Section XI requirements. The integral design requires specialized inspection approaches due to the compact configuration. RPV inspections are performed during refueling outages when the module is removed from the reactor pool.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: RPV ISI per ASME Section XI using volumetric and surface examination.
Inspections performed during refueling outages when module is removed from pool.

questions.question: How does the NuScale secondary side interface with the primary system through the steam generator?
questions.expected_answer: Feedwater enters the SG tube side at approximately 300 degrees F and is heated by the primary coolant flowing on the shell side. The helical coil design maximizes heat transfer area in the compact space. Superheated steam exits at approximately 575 degrees F. The secondary side is at a lower pressure than the primary, ensuring any tube leak results in primary-to-secondary leakage direction.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Feedwater enters SG tubes at ~300°F, steam exits at ~575°F.
Primary pressure exceeds secondary, ensuring primary-to-secondary leakage direction for any tube failure.

questions.question: Using Tables 5.1-1 and 5.1-2, what is the relationship between the RCS hot leg volume and the hot leg temperature?
questions.expected_answer: Table 5.1-1 shows the hot leg volume is 635 ft³. Table 5.1-2 shows the hot leg temperature (THot) at 100% power is 590.1 degrees F. These two values define the hot leg thermal-hydraulic conditions.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Table 5.1-1: Hot Leg volume = 635 ft³.
Table 5.1-2: THot at 100% = 590.1°F.

questions.question: Using Table 5.2-5 and Table 5.2-4, what water chemistry limits apply to the primary coolant, and what materials must they protect?
questions.expected_answer: Table 5.2-5 specifies chloride <= 0.05 ppm, fluoride <= 0.05 ppm, dissolved oxygen <= 0.005 ppm, dissolved hydrogen 25-50 cc/kg, and boron 0-2000 ppm. Table 5.2-4 shows these limits protect Alloy 690 SG tubes, SA-508 Grade 3 RPV steel, and stainless steel cladding.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Table 5.2-5: Cl≤0.05 ppm, F≤0.05 ppm, O₂≤0.005 ppm, H₂ 25-50 cc/kg, B 0-2000 ppm.
Table 5.2-4: Materials include SA-508 Grade 3, SA-533 Type B, Alloy 690, Alloy 718.

questions.question: What do Tables 5.3-3 and 5.3-8 reveal about the RPV adjusted reference temperatures and PTS screening?
questions.expected_answer: Table 5.3-3 shows adjusted reference temperatures at 57 EFPY: 92.2 degrees F for the shell, 120.4 degrees F for welds, and 77.4 degrees F for the bottom head. Table 5.3-8 shows RTPTS screening values are well below the 270 degrees F (plates/forgings) and 300 degrees F (welds) criteria.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Table 5.3-3: ART at 57 EFPY: shell 92.2°F, weld 120.4°F, bottom 77.4°F.
Table 5.3-8: RTPTS values below 270°F/300°F screening criteria.

questions.question: From Tables 5.1-1, what are the core volume and the steam generator volume in the RCS?
questions.expected_answer: Table 5.1-1 shows the core volume is 89 ft³ and the steam generator volume is 621 ft³. The SG volume is approximately 7 times larger than the core volume, reflecting the extensive helical coil tube bundle.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Table 5.1-1: Core volume = 89 ft³, SG volume = 621 ft³.

questions.question: What LTOP setpoints are specified in Table 5.2-10, and how do they relate to temperature?
questions.expected_answer: Table 5.2-10 specifies three LTOP setpoints based on RCS temperature: below 193 degrees F the setpoint is 380 psia, at 240 degrees F it is 615.5 psia, and at 277 degrees F it is 850 psia. The setpoints increase with temperature as the vessel material can tolerate higher pressures at higher temperatures.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Table 5.2-10: LTOP setpoints: <193°F→380 psia, 240°F→615.5 psia, 277°F→850 psia.

questions.question: What are the Charpy upper shelf energy values for NuScale RPV materials from Tables 5.3-9 and 5.3-10?
questions.expected_answer: Tables 5.3-9 and 5.3-10 show that the minimum Charpy upper shelf energy after irradiation is 58.4 ft-lb, which exceeds the 10 CFR 50 Appendix G requirement of 50 ft-lb minimum through the end of the design life.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Tables 5.3-9/10: Minimum Charpy USE after irradiation = 58.4 ft-lb, exceeding 50 ft-lb requirement.

questions.question: Comparing Figures 1.2-5 and 5.1-1, what internal components of the NPM are visible in the cutaway views?
questions.expected_answer: Figure 1.2-5 (Ch.01 cutaway) and Figure 5.1-1 (Ch.05 major components) both show the RPV containing the core at the bottom, helical coil SG in the middle annular region, and pressurizer space at the top. The CNV surrounds the entire RPV. The riser separates the hot leg (inside) from the cold leg (annulus around the SG).

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Figure 1.2-5: NuScale Power Module Cutaway showing internal arrangement.
Figure 5.1-1: NuScale Power Module Major Components.

questions.question: What do Figures 5.4-1 and 5.4-2 reveal about steam generator tube access for inspection?
questions.expected_answer: Figure 5.4-1 shows the helical coil SG assembly with tubes wound around the riser within the RPV annulus. Figure 5.4-2 shows the SG tube access ports at the top of the module, providing access to tube ends for inspection, plugging, and leak testing from above.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Figure 5.4-1: Steam Generator Assembly.
Figure 5.4-2: SG Tube Access from top of module.

questions.question: What does Figure 5.4-7 show about the DHRS configuration, and how do Figures 5.4-8 through 5.4-16 characterize its performance?
questions.expected_answer: Figure 5.4-7 shows the DHRS schematic with condensers submerged in the reactor pool connected to the SG secondary side. Figures 5.4-8 through 5.4-16 show cooldown performance curves demonstrating how RCS temperature and pressure decrease over time during passive cooling, with the pool absorbing decay heat.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Figure 5.4-7: DHRS Schematic showing condensers in reactor pool.
Figures 5.4-8 through 5.4-16: DHRS cooldown performance curves.

questions.question: What do Figures 1.2-2 and 1.2-3 show about the NuScale functional boundaries and the single NPM with secondary equipment?
questions.expected_answer: Figure 1.2-2 shows the NuScale functional boundaries delineating the nuclear steam supply system from the balance of plant. Figure 1.2-3 shows a schematic of a single NPM with its associated secondary equipment including the turbine-generator, condenser, and feedwater system.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Figure 1.2-2: NuScale Functional Boundaries.
Figure 1.2-3: Schematic of Single NPM and Secondary Equipment.

questions.question: What are the RCS volume components from Table 5.1-1, and how does the simplified diagram in Figure 5.1-2 show these regions?
questions.expected_answer: Table 5.1-1 lists volumes: Hot Leg 635 ft³, Cold Leg 578 ft³, Core 89 ft³, SG 621 ft³, PZR 578 ft³. Figure 5.1-2 shows these as distinct regions in the natural circulation loop: core at the bottom, riser (hot leg) going up, pressurizer at the top, SG and downcomer (cold leg) going down.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Table 5.1-1: Hot Leg 635 ft³, Cold Leg 578 ft³, Core 89 ft³, SG 621 ft³, PZR 578 ft³.
Figure 5.1-2: RCS Simplified Diagram showing flow regions.

questions.question: What are the RPV height and inner diameter from Table 5.3-1, and how does Figure 5.3-1 show the vessel cross section?
questions.expected_answer: Table 5.3-1 specifies the RPV height as 778 inches with inner diameters of 96.5, 104.5, and 106.5 inches at different elevations. Figure 5.3-1 shows the RPV cross section with varying wall thickness and diameter, the core region, downcomer annulus, and internal support structures.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Table 5.3-1: RPV height 778 in, ID 96.5/104.5/106.5 in.
Figure 5.3-1: RPV Cross Section showing varying diameter regions.

questions.question: What DHRS performance does Figure 5.4-7 schematic show, and what initial conditions from Table 5.1-2 apply?
questions.expected_answer: Figure 5.4-7 shows the DHRS schematic with passive condensers transferring heat to the reactor pool. The initial conditions from Table 5.1-2 are THot=590.1 degrees F, TCold=496.6 degrees F, and flow rate of 587 kg/s at 100% power, which define the starting thermal state for DHRS cooldown analysis.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Figure 5.4-7: DHRS Schematic.
Table 5.1-2: THot=590.1°F, TCold=496.6°F, flow=587 kg/s at 100%.

questions.question: What water chemistry specifications from Table 5.2-5 apply to the RCS, and how does the CVCS described in the text maintain them?
questions.expected_answer: Table 5.2-5 specifies Cl <= 0.05 ppm, F <= 0.05 ppm, O2 <= 0.005 ppm, H2 25-50 cc/kg, and B 0-2000 ppm. The CVCS maintains these through continuous letdown/makeup flow, ion exchange demineralizers for impurity removal, hydrogen injection for oxygen control, and boron adjustment via the boric acid system.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: CVCS uses ion exchangers, hydrogen injection, and boric acid for chemistry control.

questions.question: What material specifications from Table 5.2-4 are used for RPV construction, and what does the text say about their qualification?
questions.expected_answer: Table 5.2-4 lists SA-508 Grade 3 for forgings, SA-533 Type B for plates, Alloy 690 for SG tubes, and Alloy 718 for bolting. These materials are qualified per ASME Section III requirements with additional NRC-approved restrictions on chemistry and heat treatment to ensure adequate fracture toughness and corrosion resistance.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Table 5.2-4: SA-508 Grade 3, SA-533 Type B, Alloy 690, Alloy 718.
Materials qualified per ASME Section III with additional NRC chemistry and heat treatment restrictions.

questions.question: What fuel enrichment limit is stated in Ch.01, and what does Ch.05 say about reactivity control through boron?
questions.expected_answer: Ch.01 states fuel enrichment is less than 4.95% U-235. Ch.05 describes boron concentration ranging from 0-2000 ppm in the RCS for reactivity control, managed by the CVCS through dilution and boration.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Fuel enrichment less than 4.95% U-235.
Boron concentration 0-2000 ppm for reactivity control via CVCS.

questions.question: What does Ch.01 state about the NuScale containment design, and what specific CNV dimensions or pressure ratings does Ch.05 provide?
questions.expected_answer: Ch.01 describes the containment as a steel pressure vessel operating under vacuum during normal operation, surrounding each RPV. Ch.05 provides details on the CNV design pressure and its role in ECCS operation, where the CNV serves as the ultimate heat sink pathway through its wall to the reactor pool.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: CNV is a steel vessel operating under vacuum during normal operation.
CNV serves as containment and supports ECCS operation by providing heat transfer path to reactor pool.

questions.question: How is the NuScale seismic design described in Ch.01, and what implications does this have for RCS components per Ch.05?
questions.expected_answer: Ch.01 describes the NuScale plant as designed for seismic events with modules submerged in a below-grade reactor pool for added protection. Ch.05 notes that RCS pressure boundary components are designed to Seismic Category I requirements per ASME Section III, ensuring structural integrity during design basis earthquakes.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: NuScale modules in below-grade reactor pool for seismic protection.
RCS pressure boundary components Seismic Category I per ASME Section III.

questions.question: What does Ch.01 say about eliminated systems in NuScale, and how does Ch.05 confirm the absence of reactor coolant pumps?
questions.expected_answer: Ch.01's Table 1.3-2 lists systems not needed in NuScale including RHR, SIS, RWST, AFW, and EDG. Ch.05 confirms the RCS operates entirely by natural circulation with zero reactor coolant pumps, eliminating RCP seal LOCA and loss-of-forced-circulation accidents.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Systems not needed: RHR, SIS, RWST, AFW, EDG.
RCS operates by natural circulation with no reactor coolant pumps.

questions.question: What does Ch.01 state about the plant control room, and how does Ch.05 describe the interface with RCS instrumentation?
questions.expected_answer: Ch.01 describes a single control room that monitors and controls all 12 modules with a modular control system. Ch.05 describes RCS instrumentation including temperature, pressure, and level measurements that feed to the control room for operator monitoring and automatic protection system actuation.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Single control room monitors all 12 modules.
RCS instrumentation provides temperature, pressure, and level to control room.

questions.question: What does Ch.01 state about the NuScale spent fuel pool design, and what implications does the 24-month refueling cycle have for Ch.05 RCS operations?
questions.expected_answer: Ch.01 describes a shared spent fuel pool for all 12 modules located adjacent to the reactor pool. The 24-month refueling cycle means the RCS must be cooled down, depressurized, and the module moved to the refueling area, which involves DHRS operation (Ch.05) to achieve safe conditions for fuel handling.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Shared spent fuel pool for all 12 modules adjacent to reactor pool.
DHRS cools RCS to safe conditions for module movement and refueling.

questions.question: Compare the operating pressure listed in Table 1.2-1 with the design pressure in Table 5.3-1 for the RPV.
questions.expected_answer: Table 1.2-1 (Ch.01) lists the operating pressure as 1850 psia. Table 5.3-1 (Ch.05) lists the RPV design pressure as 2100 psia. The 250 psia margin between operating and design pressure accommodates transients and provides a safety margin.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Table 1.2-1: Operating pressure 1850 psia.
Table 5.3-1: RPV design pressure 2100 psia.

questions.question: What pressurizer volume does Table 1.3-1 give for NuScale vs a typical PWR, and how does Table 5.1-1 detail the NuScale pressurizer volume?
questions.expected_answer: Table 1.3-1 (Ch.01) shows NuScale PZR volume as 568 ft³ vs 1800 ft³ for a typical PWR. Table 5.1-1 (Ch.05) specifies the pressurizer volume as 578 ft³. The small difference reflects different measurement conventions but confirms the much smaller NuScale pressurizer.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Table 1.3-1: NuScale PZR 568 ft³ vs typical PWR 1800 ft³.
Table 5.1-1: PZR volume = 578 ft³.

questions.question: According to Table 1.3-1 and Table 5.1-2, what are the NuScale hot leg temperatures in each source?
questions.expected_answer: Table 1.3-1 (Ch.01) lists the NuScale hot leg temperature as 590 degrees F. Table 5.1-2 (Ch.05) provides more precision at 590.1 degrees F at 100% power. The values are consistent between the overview and detailed chapters.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Table 1.3-1: NuScale THot = 590°F.
Table 5.1-2: THot at 100% = 590.1°F.

questions.question: What vessel inner diameter does Table 1.3-1 give for NuScale vs a typical PWR, and how does Table 5.3-1 further detail the NuScale RPV dimensions?
questions.expected_answer: Table 1.3-1 (Ch.01) gives NuScale vessel ID as 107.5 inches vs 173 inches for a typical PWR. Table 5.3-1 (Ch.05) provides detailed dimensions: 96.5 inches (lower), 104.5 inches (middle), and 106.5 inches (upper section). The varying diameters reflect the RPV stepped shell design.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Table 1.3-1: NuScale vessel ID 107.5 in vs typical PWR 173 in.
Table 5.3-1: RPV ID 96.5/104.5/106.5 inches at different elevations.

questions.question: Compare the NuScale Power Module as shown in Figure 1.2-4 (Ch.01) with the detailed component view in Figure 5.1-1 (Ch.05). What additional detail does Ch.05 provide?
questions.expected_answer: Figure 1.2-4 shows the external module configuration with CNV and piping connections. Figure 5.1-1 provides a more detailed view showing internal components: core region, riser, SG tube bundle, pressurizer space, and their relative positions within the RPV, plus the CNV surrounding it.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Figure 1.2-4: NuScale Power Module external view.
Figure 5.1-1: Detailed internal component arrangement.

questions.question: How does the conceptual site layout in Figure 1.2-1 relate to the DHRS schematic in Figure 5.4-7 in terms of the reactor pool?
questions.expected_answer: Figure 1.2-1 shows the overall site with the reactor building containing the shared reactor pool housing up to 12 modules. Figure 5.4-7 shows how each module's DHRS condensers are submerged in this same reactor pool, connecting the plant-level layout to the module-level safety system design.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Figure 1.2-1: Site layout showing reactor building with pool.
Figure 5.4-7: DHRS condensers in reactor pool.

questions.question: Using Table 1.2-1, Table 5.1-2, and the RCS flow diagram Figure 5.1-3, describe the thermal-hydraulic operating conditions of the NuScale RCS.
questions.expected_answer: Table 1.2-1 gives 160 MWt and 1850 psia operating conditions. Table 5.1-2 details THot=590.1 degrees F, TCold=496.6 degrees F, flow=587 kg/s. Figure 5.1-3 shows the flow schematic with the natural circulation path through core, riser, SG, and downcomer with instrument locations.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Table 1.2-1: 160 MWt, 1850 psia.
Table 5.1-2: THot=590.1°F, TCold=496.6°F, flow=587 kg/s.
Figure 5.1-3: RCS Schematic Flow Diagram.

questions.question: What does Table 1.3-1 say about NuScale fuel assemblies, and how does Figure 5.1-1 show the core region within the module?
questions.expected_answer: Table 1.3-1 states NuScale has 37 fuel assemblies with 6.56 ft active fuel length, compared to 193 assemblies and 12 ft in a typical PWR. Figure 5.1-1 shows the core region at the bottom of the RPV where these fuel assemblies are located, with the riser directly above directing heated coolant upward.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Table 1.3-1: NuScale 37 assemblies, 6.56 ft fuel vs PWR 193 assemblies, 12 ft.
Figure 5.1-1: Core region at bottom of RPV.

questions.question: What safety enhancement does Table 1.2-2 attribute to the integral RCS, and how does Figure 5.1-2 illustrate this design?
questions.expected_answer: Table 1.2-2 states that housing the RCS within the RPV eliminates large-bore piping and thus eliminates large-break LOCA. Figure 5.1-2 shows the simplified RCS diagram where all components (core, SG, pressurizer) are within the single RPV, with no external loops or large-bore piping connecting separate vessels.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Table 1.2-2: RCS in RPV eliminates LBLOCA.
Figure 5.1-2: RCS simplified diagram showing all components within RPV.

questions.question: How does the NuScale natural circulation driving force compare to forced circulation in a conventional PWR?
questions.expected_answer: NuScale relies on buoyancy-driven natural circulation with no RCPs, producing lower flow rates than forced circulation PWRs. This is acceptable because the lower power density of the NuScale core requires less coolant flow for adequate heat removal.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Natural circulation provides adequate flow for heat removal at the lower NuScale power density without RCPs.

questions.question: How does the NuScale containment differ from a conventional large dry containment?
questions.expected_answer: NuScale uses a compact steel containment vessel (CNV) operating under vacuum, while conventional PWRs use large concrete dry containments at near-atmospheric pressure. The NuScale CNV is submerged in the reactor pool, providing direct heat transfer capability, while conventional containments rely on spray and fan cooler systems.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: NuScale CNV is a compact steel vessel under vacuum, submerged in reactor pool, unlike conventional large dry containments.

questions.question: How does the NuScale passive safety approach compare to active safety systems in conventional PWRs?
questions.expected_answer: NuScale uses passive safety systems that operate without AC power or operator action, relying on natural forces like gravity and natural circulation. Conventional PWRs require active pumps, diesel generators, and operator actions for emergency core cooling and heat removal.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: NuScale passive safety systems require no AC power or operator action, using gravity and natural circulation.

questions.question: How does the NuScale ECCS design differ from conventional emergency core cooling systems?
questions.expected_answer: NuScale ECCS uses valves (RVVs and RRVs) to establish communication between RPV and CNV, allowing coolant recirculation by gravity. Conventional ECCS uses multiple high and low pressure injection pumps, accumulators, and a refueling water storage tank. NuScale eliminates the need for these active injection systems.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: NuScale ECCS uses RVVs/RRVs for passive recirculation vs conventional active injection systems.

questions.question: How does NuScale's integral steam generator design differ from external U-tube steam generators?
questions.expected_answer: NuScale uses helical coil SGs integrated within the RPV annulus, with primary coolant on the shell side and feedwater/steam inside the tubes. Conventional PWRs use external U-tube SGs with primary coolant inside the tubes. The integral design eliminates large primary piping between RPV and SG.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Helical coil SGs integrated in RPV annulus eliminate large primary piping to external SGs.

questions.question: How does the NuScale single control room concept differ from conventional multi-unit plants?
questions.expected_answer: NuScale operates all 12 modules from a single control room with a modular human-machine interface, while conventional multi-unit plants typically have separate control rooms for each unit. This is enabled by the simplified passive systems that reduce operator workload per module.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Single control room for all 12 modules enabled by simplified passive design.

questions.question: According to Table 1.3-1, how does NuScale's electrical output compare to a typical PWR?
questions.expected_answer: Table 1.3-1 shows NuScale produces 50 MWe per module vs 1186 MWe for a typical PWR. The NuScale plant with 12 modules produces 570 MWe net, still less than half a single conventional PWR unit.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Table 1.3-1: NuScale 50 MWe vs typical PWR 1186 MWe.

questions.question: According to Table 1.3-1, how does NuScale's thermal power compare to a typical PWR?
questions.expected_answer: Table 1.3-1 shows NuScale thermal power is 160 MWt per module vs 3411 MWt for a typical PWR. The NuScale core is approximately 4.7% of a typical PWR thermal rating, which enables passive cooling.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Table 1.3-1: NuScale 160 MWt vs typical PWR 3411 MWt.

questions.question: Using Table 1.3-1, compare the number of fuel assemblies and active fuel length between NuScale and a typical PWR.
questions.expected_answer: Table 1.3-1 shows NuScale has 37 fuel assemblies with 6.56 ft active fuel length, while a typical PWR has 193 assemblies with 12 ft active fuel length. NuScale uses about 19% as many assemblies with 55% of the fuel length.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Table 1.3-1: NuScale 37 assemblies/6.56 ft vs PWR 193 assemblies/12 ft.

questions.question: According to Table 1.3-1, how does NuScale's design life compare to a typical PWR?
questions.expected_answer: Table 1.3-1 shows NuScale has a 60-year design life compared to 40 years for a typical PWR. The 50% longer design life reflects modern materials and design standards, plus the reduced neutron fluence on the RPV due to lower power density.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Table 1.3-1: NuScale 60 years vs typical PWR 40 years design life.

questions.question: Looking at Figure 1.2-6, how does the cutaway view of the NPM show the relative sizes of the containment and reactor vessels?
questions.expected_answer: Figure 1.2-6 shows the CNV is significantly larger than the RPV it contains, with a substantial annular gap between them. The RPV occupies the central portion while the CNV provides the external boundary. The vacuum gap between vessels serves as thermal insulation and pressure suppression volume.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Figure 1.2-6: Cutaway View of NPM showing CNV-RPV arrangement.

questions.question: Based on Figure 5.4-1, how does the helical coil SG arrangement differ visually from a conventional U-tube SG?
questions.expected_answer: Figure 5.4-1 shows the helical coil SG with tubes wound in a spiral pattern around the central riser within the RPV annulus. Unlike a conventional U-tube SG which has a separate vessel with straight/U-shaped tubes, the NuScale SG is fully integrated, compact, and uses the helical geometry to maximize heat transfer area in limited space.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Figure 5.4-1: Steam Generator Assembly showing helical coil configuration within RPV.

questions.question: Using Table 1.3-2 and the text description of NuScale safety systems, compare the systems NuScale eliminates versus those retained.
questions.expected_answer: Table 1.3-2 lists eliminated systems: RHR, SIS, RWST, AFW, EDG, and large containment spray. NuScale retains and simplifies: ECCS (passive valves instead of pumps), DHRS (passive condensers instead of AFW), and the containment (vacuum CNV instead of large dry with sprays). The passive approach eliminates more systems than it retains.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Table 1.3-2: Systems not needed: RHR, SIS, RWST, AFW, EDG.

questions.question: How does the NuScale operating pressure in Table 1.2-1 compare to the LTOP setpoints in Table 5.2-10?
questions.expected_answer: Table 1.2-1 gives the operating pressure as 1850 psia. Table 5.2-10 shows LTOP setpoints at low temperatures: 380 psia (<193 degrees F), 615.5 psia (240 degrees F), and 850 psia (277 degrees F). The LTOP setpoints are much lower than operating pressure, protecting the vessel when it is cold and less ductile.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Table 1.2-1: Operating pressure 1850 psia.

questions.question: Compare the NuScale RPV dimensions from Table 5.3-1 with the visual representation in Figure 5.3-1.
questions.expected_answer: Table 5.3-1 specifies RPV height of 778 inches, design pressure 2100 psia, design temperature 650 degrees F, and varying IDs (96.5/104.5/106.5 in). Figure 5.3-1 cross section shows the stepped vessel profile with these different diameter regions, illustrating the lower shell, transition, and upper shell sections.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Table 5.3-1: RPV 778 in height, 2100 psia, 96.5/104.5/106.5 in ID.

questions.question: Compare the NuScale DHRS passive cooling mechanism with conventional auxiliary feedwater systems.
questions.expected_answer: NuScale DHRS uses passive condensers submerged in the reactor pool, requiring no pumps, power, or operator action. Conventional AFW uses motor-driven and turbine-driven pumps with a separate water source. The DHRS eliminates multiple failure modes: pump failure, valve misposition, and loss of water supply.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: DHRS condensers in reactor pool provide passive cooling without pumps or power.
Two independent DHRS trains eliminate AFW system and associated failure modes.

questions.question: Compare the primary-to-secondary pressure relationship in NuScale vs conventional PWRs and its safety implications.
questions.expected_answer: In both NuScale and conventional PWRs, primary pressure exceeds secondary pressure, so any SG tube leak results in primary-to-secondary leakage. However, NuScale's integral design means the SG is inside the RPV, so a tube leak does not result in RCS leakage outside containment. In conventional PWRs, a tube rupture releases radioactive primary coolant to the secondary side outside containment.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Primary pressure exceeds secondary; any tube leak is primary-to-secondary direction.
Integral SG design means tube leak stays within RPV/containment boundary.

questions.question: Compare the NuScale RPV materials with those used in conventional PWR vessels.
questions.expected_answer: NuScale uses SA-508 Grade 3 Class 1 forgings and SA-533 Type B plates for the RPV, similar to conventional PWRs. However, NuScale imposes stricter chemistry limits on beltline materials (Cu <= 0.06%, Ni <= 0.85%) to minimize irradiation embrittlement over the 60-year design life.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: RPV uses SA-508 Grade 3 and SA-533 Type B, similar to conventional PWRs.
Stricter beltline chemistry: Cu≤0.06%, Ni≤0.85% for 60-year embrittlement resistance.

questions.question: Compare the NuScale pressurizer design with a conventional separate pressurizer vessel.
questions.expected_answer: NuScale's pressurizer is integral to the upper RPV head, sharing the same vessel as the core and SG. Conventional PWRs have a separate pressurizer vessel connected via a surge line. The integral design eliminates the surge line, associated piping, and potential surge line LOCA. Both use heaters and spray for pressure control.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Pressurizer integral to upper RPV head, eliminating separate vessel and surge line.
Both NuScale and conventional designs use heaters and spray for pressure control.

questions.question: How do the NuScale ECCS valve design and conventional ECCS injection systems differ in their response to a LOCA?
questions.expected_answer: NuScale ECCS valves (RVVs/RRVs) open to connect RPV to CNV, allowing steam to vent and condensed water to recirculate by gravity. Conventional ECCS activates high-pressure injection, then transitions to low-pressure injection and recirculation using pumps. NuScale's approach is fully passive while conventional systems require AC power for pump operation.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: ECCS RVVs vent steam, RRVs recirculate condensed water by gravity.
Fully passive ECCS operation without AC power or pumps.

questions.question: Compare the primary coolant flow paths during normal operation vs DHRS operation.
questions.expected_answer: During normal operation, primary coolant flows upward through the core and riser, then downward through the SG region where heat transfers to the secondary side. During DHRS operation, after reactor trip and secondary isolation, the primary still circulates by natural circulation but heat is removed via the DHRS condensers in the pool through the SG tubes.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Normal operation: natural circulation through core, riser, SG downcomer.
DHRS: heat removed via condensers in pool through SG tubes after secondary isolation.

questions.question: Compare the NuScale RPV structural integrity program with conventional PWR vessel surveillance programs.
questions.expected_answer: Both NuScale and conventional PWRs use material surveillance capsules to monitor irradiation effects on vessel material. NuScale benefits from lower neutron fluence due to smaller core power, resulting in less embrittlement. The NuScale adjusted reference temperatures at end of life remain well below PTS screening criteria, providing larger margins than many conventional plants.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Material surveillance capsules monitor irradiation effects on RPV materials.
Lower fluence results in ARTs well below PTS screening criteria with large margins.

questions.question: Compare the primary coolant chemistry control in NuScale with conventional PWR chemistry programs.
questions.expected_answer: Both NuScale and conventional PWRs use similar chemistry programs: boron for reactivity control, hydrogen for radiolysis suppression, and strict limits on contaminants. NuScale's CVCS performs the same functions but is sized for the smaller RCS volume. The key difference is NuScale's Alloy 690 SG tubes replacing Alloy 600, reducing SCC susceptibility.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Chemistry program includes boron, hydrogen, and contaminant limits similar to conventional PWRs.
Alloy 690 SG tubes replace Alloy 600 for improved SCC resistance.

questions.question: Compare the NuScale heatup and cooldown rate limitations with those of conventional PWRs.
questions.expected_answer: Both NuScale and conventional PWRs must limit heatup/cooldown rates to protect RPV integrity. NuScale P-T limits are defined in Tables 5.3-6/7 with specific curves for heatup, cooldown, and ISLH conditions. The rates are governed by fracture mechanics analysis of the RPV beltline region under thermal stress.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Heatup/cooldown rates limited by P-T curves based on fracture mechanics analysis.
P-T limits protect beltline region under thermal stress during temperature transients.

questions.question: Compare the NuScale reactor safety valve (RSV) design philosophy with conventional PWR safety valves.
questions.expected_answer: NuScale has 2 RSVs on the RPV head with setpoints of 2075/2100 psia to prevent overpressurization. Conventional PWRs have pressurizer safety valves (PSVs) on the separate pressurizer with similar functions. NuScale RSVs discharge into the containment (CNV) rather than to a relief tank, and the vacuum CNV aids in condensing discharged steam.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Two RSVs with setpoints 2075/2100 psia on RPV head, discharge into CNV.
RSV discharge into vacuum CNV where steam condenses, unlike conventional relief tanks.

questions.question: Using Tables 5.1-1 and 5.1-2, compare the hot leg and cold leg volumes with their respective temperatures.
questions.expected_answer: Table 5.1-1 shows Hot Leg volume is 635 ft³ and Cold Leg volume is 578 ft³ (hot leg is ~10% larger). Table 5.1-2 shows THot=590.1 degrees F and TCold=496.6 degrees F, a temperature difference of 93.5 degrees F. The larger hot leg volume with higher temperature provides buoyancy driving head for natural circulation.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Table 5.1-1: Hot Leg 635 ft³, Cold Leg 578 ft³.
Table 5.1-2: THot=590.1°F, TCold=496.6°F.

questions.question: Compare the 100% power flow conditions with minimum design flow conditions from Table 5.1-2.
questions.expected_answer: At 100% power: flow rate is 587 kg/s with delta-T of 99.8 degrees F. At minimum design flow (91.7%): flow is 538.5 kg/s with delta-T of 107.6 degrees F. The lower flow rate produces a larger temperature rise across the core, as expected from energy balance.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Table 5.1-2: 100% flow 587 kg/s ΔT=99.8°F; min design 91.7% 538.5 kg/s ΔT=107.6°F.

questions.question: Compare the RSV setpoints from Table 5.2-2 with the RPV design pressure from Table 5.3-1.
questions.expected_answer: Table 5.2-2 gives RSV setpoints of 2075 and 2100 psia. Table 5.3-1 gives RPV design pressure of 2100 psia. The first RSV opens at 2075 psia (25 psia below design) and the second at 2100 psia (at design pressure), ensuring pressure relief before exceeding design limits.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Table 5.2-2: RSV setpoints 2075/2100 psia.
Table 5.3-1: RPV design pressure 2100 psia.

questions.question: Compare the adjusted reference temperatures for shell, weld, and bottom head materials from Table 5.3-3.
questions.expected_answer: Table 5.3-3 at 57 EFPY shows: shell ART = 92.2 degrees F, weld ART = 120.4 degrees F, bottom head ART = 77.4 degrees F. The weld has the highest ART, making it the limiting material for P-T curve development. The bottom head has the lowest ART due to less neutron fluence.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Table 5.3-3: ART at 57 EFPY: shell 92.2°F, weld 120.4°F, bottom 77.4°F.

questions.question: Using Table 5.2-5, compare the chloride and fluoride limits with the dissolved oxygen limit in primary coolant.
questions.expected_answer: Table 5.2-5 shows chloride and fluoride limits are both 0.05 ppm, while dissolved oxygen must be below 0.005 ppm (10x stricter). The tighter oxygen control is needed to maintain reducing conditions that protect Alloy 690 SG tubes and stainless steel cladding from oxidative stress corrosion.

questions.question: Compare the SG design parameters from Table 5.4-1 with the RPV design parameters from Table 5.3-1.
questions.expected_answer: Both the SG (Table 5.4-1) and RPV (Table 5.3-1) have the same design pressure of 2100 psia and design temperature of 650 degrees F, since the SG is integral to the RPV. The SG steam outlet is ~575 degrees F and FW inlet is ~300 degrees F. The RPV overall height is 778 inches, housing both core and SG regions.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Table 5.4-1: SG design pressure 2100 psia, 650°F, steam ~575°F, FW ~300°F.
Table 5.3-1: RPV design 2100 psia, 650°F, height 778 in.

questions.question: Compare the NuScale Power Module in Figure 1.2-4 with the cutaway in Figure 1.2-5. What additional information does the cutaway reveal?
questions.expected_answer: Figure 1.2-4 shows the external Power Module with CNV outer shell and upper piping connections. Figure 1.2-5 cutaway reveals the internal arrangement: core at the bottom, helical SG tubes wrapping around the riser in the middle, and pressurizer space at the top, all within the RPV inside the CNV.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Figure 1.2-4: NuScale Power Module external view.
Figure 1.2-5: Cutaway revealing internal component arrangement.

questions.question: Compare the simplified RCS diagram (Figure 5.1-2) with the detailed schematic flow diagram (Figure 5.1-3). What additional detail does the schematic provide?
questions.expected_answer: Figure 5.1-2 shows the basic natural circulation path in simplified form. Figure 5.1-3 adds instrumentation locations, valve positions, CVCS connections, and detailed flow paths for normal operation, including letdown and charging connections.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Figure 5.1-2: RCS Simplified Diagram.
Figure 5.1-3: RCS Schematic Flow Diagram with instrumentation and CVCS connections.

questions.question: Compare the steam generator assembly (Figure 5.4-1) with the SG tube access view (Figure 5.4-2). How do they relate to maintenance?
questions.expected_answer: Figure 5.4-1 shows the complete SG assembly with helical coil tubes in the RPV annulus. Figure 5.4-2 shows the access path from the top of the module to reach SG tube ends for inspection and plugging. Together they show how the integrated design still allows maintenance access from above.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Figure 5.4-1: SG Assembly with helical coil tubes.
Figure 5.4-2: SG Tube Access from top for maintenance.

questions.question: Compare Figure 5.4-7 (DHRS Schematic) with Figures 5.4-8 through 5.4-16 (performance curves). How do the curves validate the schematic design?
questions.expected_answer: Figure 5.4-7 shows the DHRS with condensers in the reactor pool connected to SG secondary piping. Figures 5.4-8-16 show cooldown curves demonstrating that RCS temperature and pressure decrease monotonically over time to safe conditions, validating that the passive design depicted in the schematic is effective.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Figure 5.4-7: DHRS Schematic.
Figures 5.4-8/16: Performance curves showing effective passive cooldown.

questions.question: Compare the RCS volumes from Table 5.1-1 with the flow path shown in Figure 5.1-2, and explain how the volume distribution supports natural circulation.
questions.expected_answer: Table 5.1-1 shows Hot Leg 635 ft³, Cold Leg 578 ft³, Core 89 ft³, SG 621 ft³. Figure 5.1-2 shows the vertical flow loop. The large hot leg/riser volume above the core creates a tall column of heated (less dense) coolant, while the cold leg/SG downcomer provides the return path. This height difference and volume distribution maximize natural circulation driving head.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Table 5.1-1: Hot Leg 635, Cold Leg 578, Core 89, SG 621 ft³.
Figure 5.1-2: Vertical flow loop for natural circulation.

questions.question: Compare the 100% and minimum flow conditions from Table 5.1-2 and explain the impact on natural circulation as described in the text.
questions.expected_answer: Table 5.1-2 shows at 100% power flow is 587 kg/s with delta-T of 99.8 degrees F, while minimum design flow is 538.5 kg/s (91.7%) with delta-T of 107.6 degrees F. The text explains that lower flow increases core temperature rise, which actually increases the buoyancy driving force for natural circulation, providing a self-regulating characteristic.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Lower flow increases delta-T, increasing buoyancy driving force for natural circulation.

questions.question: Compare the LTOP setpoints from Table 5.2-10 with the P-T limit curves described in Tables 5.3-6/7, explaining their relationship.
questions.expected_answer: Table 5.2-10 LTOP setpoints (380/615.5/850 psia at 193/240/277 degrees F) provide overpressure protection below the P-T limit curves in Tables 5.3-6/7. The LTOP setpoints are set well below the P-T limits at each temperature to ensure that even with LTOP valve response time and overshoot, the P-T limits are not exceeded.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Table 5.2-10: LTOP setpoints 380/615.5/850 psia.
Tables 5.3-6/7: P-T limit curves for heatup/cooldown above LTOP setpoints.

questions.question: Using Table 5.3-3 and Figure 5.3-1, compare the irradiation effects on different RPV regions and explain why the weld has the highest ART.
questions.expected_answer: Table 5.3-3 shows ARTs: shell 92.2 degrees F, weld 120.4 degrees F, bottom head 77.4 degrees F. Figure 5.3-1 shows the RPV cross section where the weld in the beltline region is directly adjacent to the core at the elevation of peak neutron fluence. The weld's higher initial RTNDT plus higher fluence exposure produces the highest ART.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Table 5.3-3: ART shell 92.2°F, weld 120.4°F, bottom 77.4°F.
Figure 5.3-1: RPV cross section showing weld at beltline core elevation.

questions.question: Compare the SG design from Table 5.4-1 and Figure 5.4-1, explaining how the helical coil geometry achieves adequate heat transfer surface.
questions.expected_answer: Table 5.4-1 shows SG flow rate of ~71.4 lbm/sec per SG with steam outlet at ~575 degrees F. Figure 5.4-1 shows the helical coil tubes wound tightly in multiple layers around the riser within the RPV annulus. The helical geometry allows a very long tube length in a compact volume, maximizing heat transfer area to achieve the required thermal duty.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Table 5.4-1: ~71.4 lbm/sec per SG, steam ~575°F.
Figure 5.4-1: Helical coil tubes in compact RPV annulus.

questions.question: Compare the NuScale thermal power per module from Ch.01 with the DHRS initial power assumption from Ch.05.
questions.expected_answer: Ch.01 states each NPM produces 160 MWt. Ch.05 uses 102% of rated power (163.2 MWt) as the initial condition for DHRS analysis. The 2% margin accounts for power measurement uncertainty and ensures conservative DHRS performance evaluation.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Each NPM produces 160 MWt.
DHRS analysis assumes 102% rated thermal power.

questions.question: Compare the NuScale gross and net electrical output (Ch.01) with the reactor pool temperature constraint (Ch.05).
questions.expected_answer: Each module produces 50 MWe gross, with 12 modules yielding 570 MWe net (Ch.01). Ch.05 specifies a 140 degrees F reactor pool temperature limit. The pool must absorb rejected heat from all modules during DHRS operation while staying below this limit.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: 50 MWe gross per module, 570 MWe net for 12 modules.
Reactor pool temperature limit: 140 degrees F.

questions.question: Compare the safety enhancement from integral RCS design (Ch.01) with the specific LOCA categories eliminated per Ch.05.
questions.expected_answer: Ch.01 states the integral RCS within the RPV eliminates large-bore piping and LBLOCA. Ch.05 confirms that without external RCS loops, the largest possible break is limited to small instrument/CVCS lines, making all LOCAs small-break events manageable by ECCS and DHRS.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Integral RCS eliminates large-bore piping and LBLOCA.
Largest break limited to small instrument lines; all LOCAs are small-break events.

questions.question: Compare the NuScale passive safety approach described in Ch.01 with the specific passive systems detailed in Ch.05.
questions.expected_answer: Ch.01 describes NuScale's philosophy of passive safety using natural forces. Ch.05 details the specific implementations: natural circulation for core cooling (no RCPs), DHRS with passive condensers for decay heat removal, ECCS with gravity-driven recirculation valves, and vacuum CNV for containment pressure suppression.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: NuScale passive safety uses natural forces without AC power.
Passive systems: natural circulation, DHRS condensers, ECCS gravity valves, vacuum CNV.

questions.question: Compare the description of the NuScale SG in Ch.01's general design with the detailed performance specifications in Ch.05.
questions.expected_answer: Ch.01 describes the SG as helical coil type integrated within the RPV using a Rankine cycle. Ch.05 provides detailed specifications: design pressure 2100 psia, feedwater inlet ~300 degrees F, steam outlet ~575 degrees F, flow rate ~71.4 lbm/sec per SG, with primary flow on the shell side.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Helical coil SG in RPV using Rankine cycle.
SG: 2100 psia design, FW ~300°F, steam ~575°F, ~71.4 lbm/sec per SG.

questions.question: How does the 12-module plant concept in Ch.01 relate to the single-module DHRS analysis in Ch.05?

questions.question: Is the NuScale natural circulation design adequate for removing core heat without reactor coolant pumps?
questions.expected_answer: Yes. The natural circulation design is adequate because the NuScale core has a much lower power density than conventional PWRs, and the tall RPV with large height difference between heat source (core) and heat sink (SG) provides sufficient buoyancy-driven flow. Analysis shows 587 kg/s flow at 100% power is sufficient for heat removal.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Natural circulation provides 587 kg/s at 100% power, adequate for heat removal at NuScale's lower power density.

questions.question: Does the NuScale vacuum containment design provide adequate pressure suppression during a LOCA?
questions.expected_answer: Yes. The vacuum CNV provides effective pressure suppression because steam released during a LOCA condenses on the cool CNV inner wall, which is maintained at reactor pool water temperature. The vacuum starting condition and condensation mechanism keep containment pressure well below design limits without active spray systems.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Vacuum CNV provides pressure suppression via steam condensation on cool CNV wall.

questions.question: Based on Table 5.3-8, do the NuScale RPV materials satisfy the PTS screening criteria of 10 CFR 50.61?
questions.expected_answer: Yes. Table 5.3-8 shows that the RTPTS values for all RPV beltline materials are well below the screening criteria of 270 degrees F for plates/forgings and 300 degrees F for welds. The maximum RTPTS is for the weld at 120.4 degrees F, providing a margin of approximately 180 degrees F below the 300 degrees F weld criterion.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Table 5.3-8: RTPTS max 120.4°F for weld, well below 270/300°F criteria.

questions.question: Based on Figure 5.1-2, does the NuScale RCS flow path appear to provide adequate driving head for natural circulation?
questions.expected_answer: Yes. Figure 5.1-2 shows a tall vertical loop with the heat source (core) at the bottom and heat sink (SG) near the top, with a long riser between them. This large elevation difference between the heat source and sink maximizes the buoyancy driving head for natural circulation, which is the primary design feature enabling pump-less operation.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Figure 5.1-2: RCS diagram showing large elevation difference between core and SG.

questions.question: Based on Table 5.3-9/10 data and the text discussion of Charpy requirements, do the NuScale RPV materials maintain adequate fracture toughness through end of life?
questions.expected_answer: Yes. Tables 5.3-9/10 show minimum Charpy upper shelf energy of 58.4 ft-lb after irradiation, exceeding the 10 CFR 50 Appendix G requirement of 50 ft-lb. The text confirms that the reduced neutron fluence from NuScale's lower power density results in less embrittlement, maintaining adequate toughness margins through the 60-year design life.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Tables 5.3-9/10: Min Charpy USE 58.4 ft-lb after irradiation.

questions.question: Is the DHRS design adequate for removing decay heat following a reactor trip and loss of normal feedwater?
questions.expected_answer: Yes. The DHRS is designed with two independent trains, each capable of removing 100% of decay heat. The passive condensers in the reactor pool provide indefinite cooling capability (limited only by pool heat capacity). No AC power, operator action, or active components are required, exceeding the 72-hour coping time requirement.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Two independent DHRS trains, each removing 100% decay heat.
DHRS provides indefinite passive cooling limited only by pool capacity, no AC power needed.

questions.question: Does the NuScale ECCS design provide adequate core cooling during a loss-of-coolant accident?
questions.expected_answer: Yes. The ECCS provides adequate cooling through passive valve operation. RVVs vent steam from RPV to CNV where it condenses, and RRVs allow condensed water to return to RPV by gravity. The IAB prevents spurious operation, and fail-open design ensures operation even without power. The combined RPV-CNV system maintains long-term coolant inventory.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: ECCS: RVVs vent steam, RRVs return condensed water by gravity.
IAB prevents spurious operation; fail-open ensures function without power.

questions.question: Is the NuScale water chemistry program adequate for preventing stress corrosion cracking of Alloy 690 SG tubes?
questions.expected_answer: Yes. The chemistry program maintains low chloride (<=0.05 ppm), fluoride (<=0.05 ppm), and dissolved oxygen (<=0.005 ppm) with dissolved hydrogen at 25-50 cc/kg. These limits, combined with Alloy 690's inherent SCC resistance (thermally treated condition), provide adequate protection. Alloy 690 has demonstrated superior SCC resistance compared to the previous Alloy 600.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Chemistry limits: Cl/F≤0.05, O₂≤0.005 ppm, H₂ 25-50 cc/kg.
Alloy 690 thermally treated provides superior SCC resistance over Alloy 600.

The lower neutron fluence due to reduced core power results in adjusted reference temperatures well below regulatory screening criteria, providing substantial margin for the full 60-year life.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Structural integrity program: surveillance capsules, chemistry limits, P-T management.
Low fluence gives ART well below screening criteria with substantial margin for 60 years.

questions.question: Does the NuScale LTOP system provide adequate protection during low-temperature heatup operations?

Multiple setpoint ranges ensure protection across all low-temperature conditions. The relief capacity is adequate to prevent exceeding P-T limits during credible overpressure events including inadvertent safety injection.

questions.expected_answer: Yes. The integral pressurizer in the upper RPV head provides adequate pressure control using electric heaters and spray. The pressurizer volume accommodates RCS inventory changes during load following and transients. The integral design eliminates the surge line and associated LOCAs while maintaining the same pressure control functions as conventional designs.

The remaining small-bore connections (CVCS, instrument lines) limit break size to small-break LOCAs, which are within ECCS and DHRS mitigation capability. This is a fundamental safety improvement over conventional loop-type PWRs.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: No large-bore piping; breaks limited to small CVCS/instrument lines.
Small-break LOCAs within ECCS and DHRS mitigation capability.

Combined with strict water chemistry controls (low oxygen, controlled hydrogen), the material selection is adequate for the 60-year design life under NuScale operating conditions.

questions.question: Does the NuScale CVCS design provide adequate boron control for reactivity management throughout the fuel cycle?
