questions.question: Based on Table 5.2-5 chemistry limits and the CVCS text description, does the NuScale chemistry program adequately prevent corrosion degradation?
questions.expected_answer: Yes. Table 5.2-5 limits halides (Cl/F<=0.05 ppm), oxygen (<=0.005 ppm), and maintains hydrogen (25-50 cc/kg). The CVCS uses ion exchangers, hydrogen injection, and continuous purification to maintain these limits. This comprehensive approach prevents SCC, pitting, and general corrosion of RCS materials for the 60-year life.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: CVCS ion exchangers and hydrogen injection maintain chemistry for 60-year life.

questions.question: Based on Table 5.2-2 RSV specifications and Figure 5.2-1, is the RSV installation adequate for reliable overpressure protection?
questions.expected_answer: Yes. Table 5.2-2 specifies 2 RSVs with 63,360 lbm/hr capacity each at setpoints of 2075/2100 psia. Figure 5.2-1 shows the RSV mounted directly on the RPV head, providing a short, direct path for pressure relief without intermediate piping that could fail or restrict flow. The dual-valve arrangement provides redundancy.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Table 5.2-2: 2 RSVs, 63360 lbm/hr, setpoints 2075/2100 psia.
Figure 5.2-1: RSV mounted directly on RPV head.

questions.question: Given the Ch.01 claim that NuScale eliminates LBLOCA, does Ch.05 provide adequate technical basis for this claim?
questions.expected_answer: Yes. Ch.01 states the integral RCS eliminates large-bore piping and LBLOCA. Ch.05 confirms all RCS components are within the RPV with no external loops, and the largest possible break is limited to small instrument/CVCS connections. Ch.05's break spectrum analysis covers only small-break LOCAs, validating the Ch.01 claim.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Integral RCS eliminates LBLOCA.
Break spectrum limited to small-break; no large-bore external piping exists.

questions.question: Does the Ch.05 DHRS analysis adequately support the Ch.01 claim of passive safety without AC power?
questions.expected_answer: Yes. Ch.01 claims NuScale safety systems require no AC power. Ch.05 DHRS analysis demonstrates adequate decay heat removal using only natural circulation through passive condensers in the reactor pool. No pumps, motors, or electrical actuation (beyond initial valve opening) is required, fully supporting the passive safety claim.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Safety systems require no AC power.
DHRS uses only natural circulation and passive condensers, no AC power needed.

questions.question: Is the NuScale 60-year design life claim from Ch.01 adequately supported by the Ch.05 RPV integrity analysis?
questions.expected_answer: Yes. Ch.01 claims 60-year design life. Ch.05 analyzes RPV embrittlement to 57 EFPY, showing all ART values well below PTS screening criteria and Charpy USE above 50 ft-lb requirements. The beltline chemistry restrictions and material surveillance program provide the technical basis for 60-year vessel integrity.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: 60-year design life.
57 EFPY analysis shows adequate margins for 60-year RPV integrity.

questions.question: Does the shared reactor pool described in Ch.01 present adequate heat sink capacity for the DHRS functions described in Ch.05?
questions.expected_answer: Yes, but with constraints. Ch.01 describes a shared pool for 12 modules. Ch.05 shows DHRS heat rejection to this pool with a 140 degrees F temperature limit. The pool must absorb decay heat from potentially all 12 modules simultaneously. The pool design provides adequate capacity for this worst case, but the temperature limit constrains how long all modules can operate on DHRS.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Shared reactor pool for 12 modules.
DHRS pool temperature limit 140°F constrains multi-module DHRS operation.

questions.question: Is the NuScale approach of eliminating emergency diesel generators (Ch.01) adequately justified by the passive safety systems in Ch.05?
questions.expected_answer: Yes. Ch.01's Table 1.3-2 lists EDGs as eliminated. Ch.05 demonstrates that all safety functions (core cooling via natural circulation, decay heat removal via DHRS, ECCS via fail-open valves, containment via passive vacuum CNV) operate without AC power. The fail-safe design of ECCS valves and gravity-driven DHRS eliminates the need for backup power.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: EDGs eliminated from NuScale design.
All safety functions operate without AC power: natural circulation, DHRS, fail-open ECCS.

questions.question: Does the Ch.05 natural circulation analysis adequately justify Ch.01's claim that NuScale does not need reactor coolant pumps?
questions.expected_answer: Yes. Ch.01 lists 0 RCPs with natural circulation as the driving force. Ch.05 demonstrates that the tall RPV geometry, low power density, and temperature-driven buoyancy produce 587 kg/s at 100% power, adequate for heat removal. The self-regulating nature (lower flow increases delta-T, increasing driving force) provides inherent stability without pumps.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: 0 RCPs, natural circulation provides coolant flow.
587 kg/s at 100% with self-regulating buoyancy-driven natural circulation.

questions.question: Is the NuScale approach to seismic design (below-grade pool, Ch.01) consistent with the Ch.05 Seismic Category I classification of RCS components?
questions.expected_answer: Yes. Ch.01's below-grade reactor pool provides inherent seismic protection by reducing amplification of ground motion. Ch.05's Seismic Category I classification of all RCS pressure boundary components per ASME Section III ensures structural integrity during design basis earthquakes. Together, the site design and component qualification provide a consistent seismic protection approach.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Below-grade reactor pool for seismic protection.
RCS components Seismic Category I per ASME Section III.

questions.question: Does the single control room concept (Ch.01) present regulatory concerns given the RCS complexity described in Ch.05?
questions.expected_answer: No significant concerns. Ch.01 describes a single control room for all 12 modules. Ch.05 shows the RCS is greatly simplified with passive safety systems requiring no operator action for safety functions. The reduced operator workload per module (no RCPs, no active ECCS injection, passive DHRS) supports the single control room approach.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Single control room for 12 modules.
Passive systems with no operator action for safety reduce per-module workload.

questions.question: Is the 24-month refueling cycle (Ch.01) consistent with the Ch.05 RCS design for cooldown and depressurization?
questions.expected_answer: Yes. Ch.01 specifies 24-month refueling. Ch.05 describes the DHRS capability for controlled cooldown and the LTOP system for safe depressurization at low temperatures. The module can be safely cooled, depressurized, and disconnected for movement to the refueling area within the planned outage window.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: 24-month refueling cycle.
DHRS provides controlled cooldown; LTOP manages depressurization for refueling.

questions.question: Does the NuScale approach of having multiple small modules (Ch.01) provide safety advantages over a single large reactor, considering the Ch.05 RCS design?
questions.expected_answer: Yes. Multiple small modules provide: (1) smaller radioactive source term per module, (2) ability to shut down and cool individual modules independently, (3) shared pool provides backup heat sink, and (4) the simplified passive RCS (Ch.05) means each module is self-contained with no dependencies on other modules for safety.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: 12 small independent modules, each with self-contained safety systems.
Each module's passive RCS is self-contained with no inter-module safety dependencies.

questions.question: Is the integral SG design described in both Ch.01 and Ch.05 adequate for addressing steam generator tube rupture (SGTR) concerns?
questions.expected_answer: Yes. Ch.01 describes the integral SG within the RPV. Ch.05 confirms that because the SG is inside the RPV/containment, an SGTR does not release radioactive coolant outside containment (unlike conventional PWRs where SGTR bypasses containment). The Alloy 690 tube material further reduces SGTR likelihood.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Integral SG within RPV/containment boundary.
SG inside containment means SGTR does not bypass containment. Alloy 690 reduces probability.

questions.question: Does the NuScale design adequately address station blackout (SBO) given the Ch.01 passive design philosophy and Ch.05 safety system details?
questions.expected_answer: Yes. NuScale's passive safety systems are inherently SBO-resistant. Ch.01 states no AC power is needed for safety functions. Ch.05 confirms ECCS valves fail open, DHRS operates by natural circulation, and containment pressure suppression is passive. An SBO does not prevent any safety function from performing, eliminating SBO as a severe accident precursor.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: No AC power needed for safety functions.
ECCS fail-open, DHRS passive, CNV passive - all function during SBO.

questions.question: Is the NuScale Rankine cycle (Ch.01) appropriately matched to the SG secondary conditions described in Ch.05?
questions.expected_answer: Yes. Ch.01 describes a conventional Rankine cycle for power conversion. Ch.05 specifies the SG produces superheated steam at ~575 degrees F from ~300 degrees F feedwater, typical conditions for a Rankine cycle with moderate superheat. The conditions are well-matched for efficient turbine operation at the 50 MWe per module scale.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Conventional Rankine cycle for power conversion.
SG produces steam at ~575°F from ~300°F FW, appropriate for Rankine cycle.

questions.question: Does the NuScale design adequately address the loss of ultimate heat sink scenario given the pool design (Ch.01) and DHRS design (Ch.05)?
questions.expected_answer: Yes. Ch.01 describes the large below-grade reactor pool as the UHS. Ch.05 shows DHRS transfers heat to this pool with the 140 degrees F limit. The pool's large thermal capacity provides extended coping time. Even with loss of pool makeup, the initial pool inventory provides days of heat absorption capability for all modules.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Below-grade reactor pool serves as ultimate heat sink.
DHRS to pool with 140°F limit; large pool capacity provides extended coping.

questions.question: Based on Table 1.3-1 and Table 5.1-2, is the NuScale thermal efficiency consistent between the overview and detailed data?
questions.expected_answer: Yes. Table 1.3-1 shows 160 MWt producing 50 MWe (31.25% gross efficiency). Table 5.1-2 temperatures (THot=590.1 degrees F, TCold=496.6 degrees F) and secondary steam at ~575 degrees F are consistent with this efficiency for a Rankine cycle. The values are thermodynamically consistent.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Table 1.3-1: 160 MWt, 50 MWe.
Table 5.1-2: THot=590.1°F, TCold=496.6°F.

questions.question: Based on Table 1.2-1 and Table 5.2-2, is the operating pressure adequately protected by the reactor safety valves?
questions.expected_answer: Yes. Table 1.2-1 operating pressure is 1850 psia. Table 5.2-2 RSV setpoints are 2075/2100 psia with 63,360 lbm/hr capacity. The 225 psia margin between operating and first RSV setpoint accommodates normal transients, and the dual RSVs provide redundant overpressure protection before reaching design pressure.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Table 1.2-1: Operating pressure 1850 psia.
Table 5.2-2: RSV setpoints 2075/2100, capacity 63360 lbm/hr.

questions.question: Is the NuScale RPV design pressure (Table 5.3-1) adequate for the operating conditions listed in Table 1.2-1?
questions.expected_answer: Yes. Table 1.2-1 gives 1850 psia operating pressure. Table 5.3-1 gives 2100 psia design pressure. The 250 psia (13.5%) margin exceeds typical ASME Code requirements and provides adequate room for operational transients, pressure excursions, and instrument uncertainty.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Table 1.2-1: 1850 psia operating.
Table 5.3-1: 2100 psia design.

questions.question: Based on Table 1.3-1 (37 assemblies) and Table 5.3-2 (beltline chemistry), is the reduced core size beneficial for RPV integrity?
questions.expected_answer: Yes. The smaller 37-assembly core (Table 1.3-1 vs 193 for PWR) produces lower neutron fluence on the RPV beltline. Combined with strict chemistry limits (Table 5.3-2: Cu<=0.06%, Ni<=0.85%), this results in significantly less irradiation embrittlement than conventional PWRs, supporting the 60-year design life.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Table 1.3-1: 37 fuel assemblies.
Table 5.3-2: Cu≤0.06%, Ni≤0.85% for reduced embrittlement.

questions.question: Based on Table 1.3-2 (eliminated systems) and Table 5.4-1 (SG/DHRS parameters), does the DHRS adequately replace the eliminated AFW system?
questions.expected_answer: Yes. Table 1.3-2 lists AFW as eliminated. Table 5.4-1 shows the DHRS parameters via the SG: 2100 psia design pressure, feedwater inlet ~300 degrees F. The DHRS uses the same SG for heat transfer but routes secondary flow to passive condensers instead of requiring pump-fed auxiliary feedwater, providing a more reliable alternative.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Table 1.3-2: AFW eliminated.
Table 5.4-1: SG/DHRS at 2100 psia, using passive condensers.

questions.question: Using Table 1.3-1 (design life 60 vs 40 years) and Table 5.3-3 (ART at 57 EFPY), is the longer NuScale design life justified?
questions.expected_answer: Yes. Table 1.3-1 shows NuScale's 60-year life vs 40 for typical PWR. Table 5.3-3 ART at 57 EFPY (max 120.4 degrees F) is well below PTS criteria (270/300 degrees F), providing ~150 degrees F margin. This large margin confirms the vessel can safely operate for 60 years without approaching embrittlement limits.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Table 1.3-1: 60-year design life.
Table 5.3-3: ART max 120.4°F at 57 EFPY, ~150°F below criteria.

questions.question: Based on Table 1.2-2 (vacuum CNV) and Table 5.2-4 (materials), does the material selection adequately support the containment design?
questions.expected_answer: Yes. Table 1.2-2 identifies vacuum CNV as a safety feature. Table 5.2-4 materials (SA-508 Grade 3, SA-533 Type B for pressure boundary, stainless steel cladding) are proven for nuclear containment applications. The materials resist corrosion from the pool water on the outside and maintain integrity under vacuum and accident pressure conditions.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Table 1.2-2: Vacuum CNV safety feature.
Table 5.2-4: SA-508, SA-533, stainless steel for containment.

questions.question: Based on Table 1.3-1 operating parameters and Table 5.1-1 RCS volumes, is the NuScale coolant inventory adequate for the module's operating conditions?
questions.expected_answer: Yes. Table 1.3-1 gives 160 MWt at 1850 psia. Table 5.1-1 shows total RCS volume approximately 2500 ft³. The ratio of coolant inventory to thermal power is higher than many conventional PWRs (1800 ft³ PZR alone in PWR vs entire NuScale PZR of 578 ft³), providing adequate thermal inertia for the lower power density.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Table 1.3-1: 160 MWt, 1850 psia.
Table 5.1-1: Total RCS ~2500 ft³.

questions.question: Based on Table 1.2-1 (12 modules, 160 MWt each) and Table 5.4-1 (SG parameters), is the SG sizing adequate for full-power heat transfer?
questions.expected_answer: Yes. Table 1.2-1 shows each module at 160 MWt. Table 5.4-1 specifies ~71.4 lbm/sec per SG with steam at ~575 degrees F and FW at ~300 degrees F. The enthalpy rise across this temperature range at the specified flow rate, combined with the helical coil heat transfer enhancement, provides adequate thermal duty for 160 MWt removal.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Table 1.2-1: 160 MWt per module.
Table 5.4-1: ~71.4 lbm/sec, steam ~575°F, FW ~300°F.

questions.question: Based on Figure 1.2-1 (site layout) and Figure 5.4-7 (DHRS), is the reactor pool arrangement adequate for multi-module DHRS operation?
questions.expected_answer: Yes. Figure 1.2-1 shows the large reactor pool housing up to 12 modules with significant water volume. Figure 5.4-7 shows each module's DHRS condensers submerged in this pool. The large pool provides adequate thermal mass to absorb decay heat from multiple modules simultaneously while remaining below the 140 degrees F limit.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Figure 1.2-1: Large reactor pool for 12 modules.
Figure 5.4-7: DHRS condensers in shared pool.

questions.question: Based on Figure 1.2-5 (cutaway) and Figure 5.1-2 (RCS diagram), is the vertical RPV arrangement adequate for establishing natural circulation?
questions.expected_answer: Yes. Figure 1.2-5 cutaway shows the tall vertical RPV with core at bottom and SG near top, providing large elevation difference. Figure 5.1-2 shows the resulting circulation loop. The vertical arrangement maximizes buoyancy driving head between heat source and sink, which is the fundamental requirement for reliable natural circulation.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Figure 1.2-5: Tall vertical RPV with core at bottom, SG near top.
Figure 5.1-2: Natural circulation loop with large elevation difference.

questions.question: Based on Figure 1.2-3 (NPM with secondary equipment) and Figure 5.4-1 (SG assembly), is the interface between primary and secondary systems adequately designed?
questions.expected_answer: Yes. Figure 1.2-3 shows the NPM connected to its turbine-generator and condenser via steam and feedwater piping. Figure 5.4-1 shows the helical coil SG inside the RPV where this energy transfer occurs. The interface is well-defined at the SG tube boundaries, maintaining primary/secondary separation within the containment boundary.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Figure 1.2-3: NPM connected to turbine/condenser.
Figure 5.4-1: SG assembly providing primary/secondary interface.

questions.question: Based on Figure 1.2-2 (functional boundaries) and Figure 5.1-3 (RCS schematic), are the NSSS boundaries appropriately defined for safety classification?
questions.expected_answer: Yes. Figure 1.2-2 delineates NSSS from balance of plant. Figure 5.1-3 shows the RCS schematic with all safety-related components within the NSSS boundary. The boundary is drawn at the containment penetrations (steam, feedwater, CVCS lines), ensuring all safety-significant RCS components are within the classified boundary.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Figure 1.2-2: NSSS/BOP functional boundaries.
Figure 5.1-3: RCS schematic with safety components within NSSS.

questions.question: Based on Figure 1.2-6 (NPM cutaway) and Figure 5.3-1 (RPV cross section), is the annular gap between RPV and CNV adequate for the vacuum containment function?
questions.expected_answer: Yes. Figure 1.2-6 shows a substantial annular gap between the RPV and CNV. Figure 5.3-1 shows the RPV outer dimensions. The annular space provides adequate volume for steam condensation during a LOCA and maintains the vacuum insulation barrier during normal operation. The gap also allows for thermal expansion of the RPV without contacting the CNV.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Figure 1.2-6: NPM cutaway showing RPV-CNV annular gap.
Figure 5.3-1: RPV cross section showing outer dimensions.

questions.question: Is the NuScale overall safety case from Ch.01 adequately supported by the detailed engineering in Ch.05, considering both tabular data and diagrams?
questions.expected_answer: Yes. Ch.01 claims passive safety without AC power for a 12-module 570 MWe plant. Ch.05 provides quantitative support: Table 5.1-2 confirms adequate natural circulation (587 kg/s), Figure 5.4-7 shows passive DHRS, Table 5.3-3 confirms RPV integrity (ART well below limits), and text confirms all safety systems function without AC power. The detailed engineering validates the high-level safety claims.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Passive safety, no AC power, 12-module plant.
Table 5.1-2: Natural circulation 587 kg/s.
Figure 5.4-7: Passive DHRS design.

questions.question: Using Table 1.2-1 plant parameters, Table 5.3-1 RPV design, and Figure 5.3-1 cross section, is the RPV adequately designed for the stated operating conditions?
questions.expected_answer: Yes. Table 1.2-1 gives 160 MWt at 1850 psia. Table 5.3-1 specifies RPV design at 2100 psia, 650 degrees F, 778 inches height. Figure 5.3-1 shows adequate wall thickness with the stepped diameter design. The 250 psia margin and 60 degrees F temperature margin provide adequate design basis with ASME Section III code compliance.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Table 1.2-1: 160 MWt, 1850 psia.
Figure 5.3-1: RPV cross section with adequate wall thickness.

questions.question: Based on Table 1.3-1 comparison, Table 5.2-4 materials, and Figure 5.4-1 SG design, does the NuScale SG offer safety advantages over conventional PWR steam generators?
questions.expected_answer: Yes. Table 1.3-1 shows the integral design vs conventional external SGs. Table 5.2-4 specifies Alloy 690 tubes (superior to Alloy 600). Figure 5.4-1 shows the helical coil design within the RPV. The integral location means SGTR does not bypass containment, Alloy 690 reduces tube degradation, and the compact design eliminates external SG piping failures.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Table 1.3-1: NuScale integral SG vs PWR external SG.
Table 5.2-4: Alloy 690 SG tubes.
Figure 5.4-1: Helical coil SG inside RPV.

questions.expected_answer: Yes. Table 1.2-2 lists passive safety features. Table 5.2-10 provides LTOP setpoints (380/615.5/850 psia) for low-temperature protection. Ch.05 text describes P-T limit curves that the LTOP protects against. Combined with RSV protection at operating temperatures, the multi-layer overpressure strategy provides adequate protection across all temperature ranges.

Table 5.2-10: LTOP setpoints 380/615.5/850 psia.
P-T limit curves protected by LTOP and RSV across temperature range.

questions.expected_answer: Yes. Table 1.3-2 lists RHR, SIS, RWST, AFW, and EDG as eliminated. Figure 5.4-7 shows the DHRS passive alternative with condensers in the pool. Figures 5.4-8-16 performance curves demonstrate the DHRS achieves safe cooldown. The passive approach eliminates failure modes of pumps, diesel generators, and active valves while achieving equivalent safety functions.

Figure 5.4-7: DHRS passive condensers.

questions.expected_answer: Yes. Table 1.3-1 claims 60 years vs 40 for PWR. Table 5.3-3 shows ART at 57 EFPY (max 120.4 degrees F). Table 5.3-8 confirms RTPTS well below 270/300 degrees F screening criteria with ~150 degrees F margin. Ch.05 text describes surveillance capsules and strict chemistry controls. Table 5.3-3: ART max 120.4°F.

questions.expected_answer: Yes. Ch.01 describes a 160 MWt module with passive cooling. Table 5.1-2 shows 587 kg/s at 100% and 538.5 kg/s at minimum flow. Figure 5.1-2 shows the vertical loop maximizing buoyancy. Ch.05 text confirms the self-regulating natural circulation and the DHRS backup. Multiple defense-in-depth layers (normal operation, DHRS, ECCS) provide adequate safety margins at all power levels.

Table 5.1-2: 587 kg/s at 100%, 538.5 kg/s at min.
